advantages: Fixtures eliminate duplication and make tests easier to read.
`yield` fixtures keep setup and teardown adjacent — easier to maintain.
Mocks make slow / flaky / costly dependencies usable in unit tests.
Built-in fixtures (`tmp_path`, `monkeypatch`, `caplog`) cover most needs without extra libraries.
`patch` lets you inject test doubles without changing production code structure.
disadvantages: Wider fixture scopes can hide cross-test pollution and create flakiness.
Overly clever fixture chains become hard to follow ('which conftest defines this?').
Mocks drift from reality — passing mock tests next to broken production code.
Heavy patching can be a design smell — code is too coupled to test cleanly.
`patch` paths are brittle — they hard-code module names that shift on refactor.
